text: Persistent gastrocutaneous fistulas can be a complication after feeding tubes that have been placed are ultimately removed. Surgical closure is occasionally required.

Previously, this was done through a midline incision approximately six to eight inches in length. However, with the assistance of robotic surgery, this is now easily and safely done through three small incisions.
The surgeon isolates the stomach from surrounding adhesions, with visible transection of vessels and minor bleeding due to the organ's rich vascularization.
The surgeon uses a stapler to seal and transect the isolated portion of the stomach, which is visibly connected to the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon leaves the staple line exposed, noting that leaks are uncommon, but proceeds to imbricate the staple line using a running monofilament suture.
The surgeon dissects and removes the remnant of the stomach that is connected to the anterior abdominal wall, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues before extracting it from the abdomen.
The surgeon removes all suture material and closes the single twelve millimeter port site, ensuring the incision is securely sealed.
As there is no large midline incision, the potential for an incisional hernia is nearly eliminated.
